Verbose only adds `rendered_content`.\\n6. **`find` narrowing doesn't re-anchor when no sub-scope is detected.** AC-6.1 requires position resolution relative to the found line, not the original scope.\\n7. **`scope: line` auto-adjusts indentation, diverging from inject-after behavior.** AC-7.12 says \\\"SHALL behave identically to inject's `after` mode.\\\"\\n8. **`find_opening` doesn't skip strings/comments when searching for the opening delimiter.** AC-4.6/4.7 — only the nesting-depth loop has string/comment awareness.\\n9. **`AfterLastMethod` regex too narrow for Rust.** Misses `const fn`, `unsafe fn`, `pub(crate) fn`, `extern \\\"C\\\" fn`.\\n10. **Location string uses `Debug` not `Display`.** Produces `classbody` instead of `class_body`.\\n11. **Mixed tab/space scope detection uses length, not literal prefix comparison.** AC-3.8 requires character-by-character whitespace comparison.\\n\\n### Minor\\n\\n12. **`AfterLastField` regex requires leading whitespace (`\\\\s+` not `\\\\s*`).** Fails on top-level fields (Go structs).\\n13. **Regex recompiled at runtime in replace.** Pre-compile during recipe parsing.\\n14. **`chars().count()` called on every scanner step.** O(n^2) per line in delimiter detection.\\n\\n## Execution Order\\n\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n21:{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_8\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\"sed -n '1,260p' docs/workstreams/workflows/SPEC.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"# SPEC.md\\n\\n> Workstream: workflows\\n> Last updated: 2026-04-04\\n> Scope: v0.3 (Phase H from ARCHITECTURE.md)\\n\\n## Overview\\n\\nThe workflows workstream adds multi-recipe orchestration to jig. A workflow is a YAML file that chains multiple recipes into a single invocation with conditional steps, variable mapping between steps, and configurable error handling. This is the composition layer — where individual recipes (create, inject, replace, patch) are combined into meaningful cross-file operations like \\\"add a field to a model and propagate it through the service, schema, admin, and tests.\\\"\\n\\nWorkflows replace the earlier `includes` concept from the spec. Unlike static includes (which always run all sub-recipes), workflows are conditional, configurable, and report per-step results. They are the prerequisite for libraries (v0.4), which define workflows over library-namespaced recipes.\\n\\nOut of scope for this workstream: library integration (v0.4), cross-library workflows (v0.9), schema-first generation (v0.9), nested workflows (a step cannot reference another workflow), and output variable passing between steps (steps share the filesystem, not variables).\\n\\n## Requirements\\n\\n### Functional Requirements\\n\\n#### FR-1: Workflow Definition Parsing\\n\\nParse a workflow YAML file into a validated internal representation. A workflow declares shared variables, an ordered list of steps (each referencing a recipe), and a default error handling mode. Resolve recipe paths relative to the workflow file location. Distinguish workflow files from recipe files by structural content.\\n\\n**Acceptance Criteria (EARS):**\\n| ID | Type | Criterion | Traces To |\\n|----|------|-----------|-----------|\\n| AC-1.1 | Event | WHEN a valid workflow YAML is provided with `steps` (required), and optionally `name`, `description`, `variables`, and `on_error`, the system SHALL parse it into a Workflow struct | TEST-1.1 |\\n| AC-1.2 | Event | WHEN a workflow step declares `recipe` (required), and optionally `when`, `vars_map`, `vars`, and `on_error`, the system SHALL parse all fields into a WorkflowStep struct | TEST-1.2 |\\n| AC-1.3 | Event | WHEN a workflow step's `recipe` path is relative, the system SHALL resolve it relative to the workflow file's directory (consistent with recipe-relative template resolution, I-7) | TEST-1.3 |\\n| AC-1.4 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is omitted from the workflow, the system SHALL default to `stop` | TEST-1.4 |\\n| AC-1.5 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is specified at the workflow level, the system SHALL accept one of: `stop`, `continue`, or `report` | TEST-1.5 |\\n| AC-1.6 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is specified at the step level, the system SHALL use the step-level value as an override for that step only | TEST-1.6 |\\n| AC-1.7 | Unwanted | IF a step's `recipe` path (resolved relative to the workflow file) does not point to an existing file, the system SHALL exit with code 1 reporting the missing recipe path and the resolved location | TEST-1.7 |\\n| AC-1.8 | Unwanted | IF the workflow YAML is malformed (invalid YAML syntax), the system SHALL exit with code 1 and an error message identifying the parse failure location | TEST-1.8 |\\n| AC-1.9 | Event | WHEN the workflow has an empty `steps: []` array, the system SHALL exit 0 with an empty steps result and no files written | TEST-1.9 |\\n| AC-1.10 | Event | WHEN the workflow YAML has no `variables` key or an empty `variables` map, the system SHALL accept the workflow as valid (no workflow-level variable declarations) | TEST-1.10 |\\n| AC-1.11 | Unwanted | IF a step specifies an invalid `on_error` value (not one of stop/continue/report), the system SHALL exit with code 1 reporting the invalid value and the allowed options | TEST-1.11 |\\n| AC-1.12 | Event | WHEN a step's `when` field is specified, the system SHALL store it as a Jinja2 template string for evaluation at execution time | TEST-1.12 |\\n| AC-1.13 | Event | WHEN a step's `vars_map` is specified, the system SHALL parse it as a string-to-string mapping (workflow variable name to recipe variable name) | TEST-1.13 |\\n| AC-1.14 | Event | WHEN a step's `vars` is specified, the system SHALL parse it as a string-to-JSON-value mapping of variable overrides | TEST-1.14 |\\n| AC-1.15 | Ubiquitous | The system SHALL distinguish workflow YAML from recipe YAML by the presence of `steps` (workflow) vs `files` (recipe) when auto-detecting in `jig validate` and `jig vars` | TEST-1.15 |\\n| AC-1.16 | Unwanted | IF a YAML file contains both `steps` and `files` top-level keys, the system SHALL exit with code 1 reporting the ambiguous file type | TEST-1.16 |\\n| AC-1.17 | Unwanted | IF a YAML file passed to `jig validate` or `jig vars` contains neither `steps` nor `files`, the system SHALL exit with code 1 reporting the missing structural key | TEST-1.17 |\\n| AC-1.18 | Event | WHEN validating a workflow, the system SHALL also parse and validate each referenced recipe file (confirming they are structurally valid recipes) | TEST-1.18 |\\n| AC-1.19 | Unwanted | IF a referenced recipe file is structurally invalid (malformed YAML, missing template files, etc.), the system SHALL exit with code 1 reporting the recipe validation error and which step referenced it | TEST-1.19 |\\n| AC-1.20 | Event | WHEN the workflow YAML has optional metadata fields (`name`, `description`), the system SHALL accept workflows with or without them | TEST-1.20 |\\n\\n#### FR-2: Workflow Variable Handling\\n\\nAccept variables from the same sources as recipe execution (--vars, --vars-file, --vars-stdin). Validate against workflow-level declarations. Pass all workflow-level variables to every step by default (shared variables).\\n\\n**Acceptance Criteria (EARS):**\\n| ID | Type | Criterion | Traces To |\\n|----|------|-----------|-----------|\\n| AC-2.1 | Event | WHEN workflow-level variables are declared, the system SHALL validate provided variables against declarations using the same rules as recipe variable validation (type checking, required fields, defaults, enum validation) | TEST-2.1 |\\n| AC-2.2 | Event | WHEN `--vars`, `--vars-file`, and/or `--vars-stdin` are provided, the system SHALL merge with the same precedence as recipes: workflow defaults < vars-file < vars-stdin < inline --vars | TEST-2.2 |\\n| AC-2.3 | Ubiquitous | The system SHALL pass all workflow-level resolved variables to every step by default (shared variables) | TEST-2.3 |\\n| AC-2.4 | Ubiquitous | The system SHALL accept variable input containing keys not declared in the workflow's variables section without error or warning (consistent with recipe behavior) | TEST-2.4 |\\n| AC-2.5 | Event | WHEN `jig vars` is invoked with a workflow file, the system SHALL output the workflow's variable declarations in the same JSON format as recipe variable output | TEST-2.5 |\\n| AC-2.6 | Event | WHEN a workflow declares no variables and no variable input is provided, the system SHALL pass an empty object to all steps | TEST-2.6 |\\n| AC-2.7 | Ubiquitous | The system SHALL accumulate all workflow-level variable validation errors and report them together with exit code 4 (consistent with recipe behavior) | TEST-2.7 |\\n\\n#### FR-3: Conditional Step Evaluation\\n\\nEvaluate `when` expressions to determine whether a step should execute. The `when` field is a Jinja2 template rendered with workflow-level variables. The rendered result determines truthiness.\\n\\n**Acceptance Criteria (EARS):**\\n| ID | Type | Criterion | Traces To |\\n|----|------|-----------|-----------|\\n| AC-3.1 | Event | WHEN a step has a `when` field, the system SHALL render it as a Jinja2 template using the workflow-level resolved variables (before vars_map and vars are applied) | TEST-3.1 |\\n| AC-3.2 | Event | WHEN the rendered `when` result, after stripping leading and trailing whitespace, is empty, case-insensitively equals \\\"false\\\", or equals \\\"0\\\", the system SHALL skip the step | TEST-3.2 |\\n| AC-3.3 | Event | WHEN the rendered `when` result is truthy (does not match any falsy pattern in AC-3.2), the system SHALL execute the step | TEST-3.3 |\\n| AC-3.4 | Event | WHEN a step is skipped due to a `when` condition evaluating to falsy, the system SHALL report the step with status \\\"skipped\\\" and include the reason \\\"when condition evaluated to false\\\" | TEST-3.4 |\\n| AC-3.5 | Event | WHEN a step has no `when` field, the system SHALL execute it unconditionally | TEST-3.5 |\\n| AC-3.6 | Unwanted | IF the `when` template references an undefined variable, the system SHALL treat it as a step failure (template rendering error), subject to the applicable on_error mode | TEST-3.6 |\\n| AC-3.7 | Unwanted | IF the `when` template has a Jinja2 syntax error, the system SHALL treat it as a step failure (template rendering error), subject to the applicable on_error mode | TEST-3.7 |\\n| AC-3.8 | Event | WHEN a `when` expression uses Jinja2 control flow (e.g., `{% if methods | length > 0 %}yes{% endif %}`), the system SHALL evaluate it correctly — the rendered output determines truthiness, not the expression syntax | TEST-3.8 |\\n\\n#### FR-4: Variable Mapping and Overrides\\n\\nAllow steps to rename and override variables when passing them to a recipe. This enables workflow variables to be mapped to different names expected by different recipes, and allows step-specific value overrides.\\n\\n**Acceptance Criteria (EARS):**\\n| ID | Type | Criterion | Traces To |\\n|----|------|-----------|-----------|\\n| AC-4.1 | Event | WHEN a step specifies `vars_map` with entries `{source: target}`, the system SHALL rename workflow variable `source` to `target` in the variables passed to the step's recipe | TEST-4.1 |\\n| AC-4.2 | Event | WHEN `vars_map` renames a variable, the original name SHALL NOT be present in the step's resolved variables (rename semantics, not copy) | TEST-4.2 |\\n| AC-4.3 | Event | WHEN a step specifies `vars`, the system SHALL merge these values into the step's variables, overriding any existing values (including values resulting from vars_map renaming) | TEST-4.3 |\\n| AC-4.4 | Ubiquitous | The variable resolution order for each step SHALL be: (1) start with all workflow-level resolved variables, (2) apply vars_map renaming, (3) apply vars overrides | TEST-4.4 |\\n| AC-4.5 | Event | WHEN the step's resolved variables are passed to the recipe, the system SHALL validate them against the recipe's variable declarations (type checking, required fields, defaults) | TEST-4.5 |\\n| AC-4.6 | Unwanted | IF a step's recipe declares a required variable that is not present in the step's resolved variables (after vars_map and vars), the system SHALL treat it as a step failure (variable validation error), subject to the applicable on_error mode | TEST-4.6 |\\n| AC-4.7 | Event | WHEN `vars_map` references a source variable that does not exist in the workflow variables, the system SHALL silently ignore that mapping entry (no error — the variable simply is not in the set) | TEST-4.7 |\\n| AC-4.8 | Event | WHEN `vars_map` maps to a target name that already exists in the workflow variables, the renamed value SHALL override the existing value for that target name | TEST-4.8 |\\n| AC-4.9 | Event | WHEN a step has neither `vars_map` nor `vars`, the system SHALL pass the workflow-level variables to the recipe unchanged | TEST-4.9 |\\n| AC-4.10 | Event | WHEN multiple vars_map entries are specified, all renamings SHALL be applied simultaneously (not sequentially — renaming `a` to `b` and `b` to `c` does not chain; the original `a` becomes `b` and the original `b` becomes `c`) | TEST-4.10 |\\n\\n#### FR-5: Error Handling Modes\\n\\nControl workflow behavior when a step fails. Three modes determine whether the workflow stops, continues silently, or continues with partial failure reporting.\\n\\n**Acceptance Criteria (EARS):**\\n| ID | Type | Criterion | Traces To |\\n|----|------|-----------|-----------|\\n| AC-5.1 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is `stop` (default) and a step fails, the system SHALL stop the workflow immediately and not execute subsequent steps | TEST-5.1 |\\n| AC-5.2 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is `stop` and a step fails, the system SHALL exit with the failed step's exit code (1, 2, 3, or 4 depending on the failure type) | TEST-5.2 |\\n| AC-5.3 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is `continue` and a step fails, the system SHALL record the failure and proceed to execute the next step | TEST-5.3 |\\n| AC-5.4 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is `continue` and one or more steps failed, the system SHALL exit with code 0 | TEST-5.4 |\\n| AC-5.5 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is `report` and a step fails, the system SHALL record the failure and proceed to execute the next step | TEST-5.5 |\\n| AC-5.6 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is `report` and one or more steps failed, the system SHALL exit with code 3 (file operation error) | TEST-5.6 |\\n| AC-5.7 | Event | WHEN a step has its own `on_error` value, the system SHALL use the step-level value for that step instead of the workflow-level default | TEST-5.7 |\\n| AC-5.8 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is `stop` and a step fails, the JSON output SHALL include results for all steps up to and including the failed step, with subsequent steps absent from the output | TEST-5.8 |\\n| AC-5.9 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is `continue` or `report` and a step fails, the step's result SHALL include the error details and any operations that completed before the failure within that step | TEST-5.9 |\\n| AC-5.10 | Event | WHEN all steps succeed (or are skipped via `when`), the system SHALL exit with code 0 regardless of the on_error mode | TEST-5.10 |\\n\\n#### FR-6: Workflow Execution\\n\\nExecute workflow steps sequentially, sharing filesystem state between steps. Each step loads and runs a complete recipe with the step's resolved variables.\\n\\n**Acceptance Criteria (EARS):**\\n| ID | Type | Criterion | Traces To |\\n|----|------|-----------|-----------|\\n| AC-6.1 | Ubiquitous | The system SHALL execute workflow steps in the order they appear in the `steps` array | TEST-6.1 |\\n| AC-6.2 | Event | WHEN a step executes, the system SHALL load the referenced recipe, render all templates with the step's resolved variables, and execute all operations in the recipe in declaration order | TEST-6.2 |\\n| AC-6.3 | Event | WHEN a step creates or modifies files, subsequent steps SHALL see those changes on the filesystem (steps share real filesystem state in normal execution mode) | TEST-6.3 |\\n| AC-6.4 | Event | WHEN `--dry-run` is specified, the system SHALL carry `virtual_files` state across steps so that a later step can read files \\\"created\\\" by an earlier step's create operation | TEST-6.4 |\\n| AC-6.5 | Event | WHEN `--force` is specified, the system SHALL pass the force flag to every step's recipe execution | TEST-6.5 |\\n| AC-6.6 | Event | WHEN `--base-dir` is specified, the system SHALL resolve all recipe output paths (to, inject, replace, patch) relative to the base directory across all steps | TEST-6.6 |\\n| AC-6.7 | Event | WHEN a recipe within a step has template paths (to, inject, replace, patch fields containing Jinja2 expressions), the system SHALL render those paths with the step's resolved variables | TEST-6.7 |\\n| AC-6.8 | Event | WHEN a recipe within a step has `skip_if` guards, the system SHALL evaluate them in the context of the current filesystem state (which may include changes from earlier steps) | TEST-6.8 |\\n| AC-6.9 | Ubiquitous | The system SHALL validate workflow-level variables before executing any step. If workflow-level validation fails (exit code 4), no steps execute | TEST-6.9 |\\n\\n#### FR-7: CLI Workflow Command\\n\\nAdd a `jig workflow` subcommand to execute workflows. Extend `jig validate` and `jig vars` to auto-detect and handle workflow files.\\n\\n**Acceptance Criteria (EARS):**\\n| ID | Type | Criterion | Traces To |\\n|----|------|-----------|-----------|\\n| AC-7.1 | Event | WHEN `jig workflow <path>` is invoked with a path to a workflow YAML file and variable sources, the system SHALL parse and execute the workflow | TEST-7.1 |\\n| AC-7.2 | Ubiquitous | The `jig workflow` command SHALL accept the same global options as `jig run`: `--vars`, `--vars-file`, `--vars-stdin`, `--dry-run`, `--json`, `--quiet`, `--force`, `--base-dir`, `--verbose` | TEST-7.2 |\\n| AC-7.3 | Event | WHEN `jig validate` is invoked with a workflow YAML file (auto-detected by presence of `steps`), the system SHALL validate the workflow structure, all step configurations, and all referenced recipes | TEST-7.3 |\\n| AC-7.4 | Event | WHEN `jig vars` is invoked with a workflow YAML file (auto-detected by presence of `steps`), the system SHALL output the workflow's variable declarations in JSON format | TEST-7.4 |\\n| AC-7.5 | Event | WHEN `jig validate` succeeds on a workflow, the human output SHALL report: workflow name (if present), variable summary, step count (unconditional vs conditional), and recipe validation status for each step | TEST-7.5 |\\n| AC-7.6 | Event | WHEN `jig validate` succeeds on a workflow, the JSON output SHALL include: `type: \\\"workflow\\\"`, `valid: true`, workflow name, variables, and a steps array with recipe path, valid status, and conditional flag for each step | TEST-7.6 |\\n| AC-7.7 | Unwanted | IF the path passed to `jig workflow` does not exist, the system SHALL exit with code 1 reporting the missing file | TEST-7.7 |\\n| AC-7.8 | Unwanted | IF the file passed to `jig workflow` is a recipe (has `files` not `steps`), the system SHALL exit with code 1 reporting \\\"expected a workflow file (with `steps`), got a recipe file (with `files`). Use `jig run` to execute recipes.\\\" | TEST-7.8 |\\n\\n#### FR-8: Workflow Output Formatting\\n\\nReport workflow execution results as JSON (stdout) or human-readable text (stderr) with per-step detail. Follow the same dual-stream output conventions as recipe execution (I-8).\\n\\n**Acceptance Criteria (EARS):**\\n| ID | Type | Criterion | Traces To |\\n|----|------|-----------|-----------|\\n| AC-8.1 | Event | WHEN JSON output is produced for a workflow, the system SHALL include a `steps` array where each element contains the step's recipe path, status, and (for executed steps) the operations array | TEST-8.1 |\\n| AC-8.2 | Event | WHEN a step succeeds, its JSON result SHALL include: `recipe` (path), `status: \\\"success\\\"`, `operations` (array of OpResult), `files_written` (array), `files_skipped` (array) | TEST-8.2 |\\n| AC-8.3 | Event | WHEN a step is skipped due to a `when` condition, its JSON result SHALL include: `recipe` (path), `status: \\\"skipped\\\"`, `reason` (string explaining the skip) | TEST-8.3 |\\n| AC-8.4 | Event | WHEN a step fails (in continue/report modes), its JSON result SHALL include: `recipe` (path), `status: \\\"error\\\"`, `error` (structured error with what/where/why/hint), `rendered_content` (if available), and `operations` (any that completed before the failure) | TEST-8.4 |\\n| AC-8.5 | Event | WHEN JSON output is produced, the system SHALL include aggregate `files_written` and `files_skipped` arrays combining results across all executed steps. A path appears in aggregate `files_written` if any step wrote to it. A path appears in aggregate `files_skipped` only if every operation targeting it across all steps was skipped | TEST-8.5 |\\n| AC-8.6 | Event | WHEN JSON output is produced, the system SHALL include a top-level `on_error` field showing the workflow-level error handling mode | TEST-8.6 |\\n| AC-8.7 | Event | WHEN JSON output is produced, the system SHALL include a top-level `workflow` field with the workflow name (or `null` if unnamed) | TEST-8.7 |\\n| AC-8.8 | Event | WHEN human output is produced, the system SHALL print step progress headers (e.g., \\\"Step 1/3: model/add-field/recipe.yaml\\\"), nested operation results indented under each step, and a summary line reporting steps executed, skipped, and failed | TEST-8.8 |\\n| AC-8.9 | Event | WHEN `--verbose` is specified, the system SHALL include rendered template content for all operations across all steps (consistent with recipe --verbose behavior) | TEST-8.9 |\\n| AC-8.10 | Event | WHEN JSON output is produced, the system SHALL include a top-level `dry_run` boolean field | TEST-8.10 |\\n| AC-8.11 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is `report` and one or more steps failed, the JSON output SHALL include a top-level `status` field of `\\\"partial\\\"`. WHEN all steps succeed (or are skipped via `when`), the `status` field SHALL be `\\\"success\\\"` | TEST-8.11 |\\n| AC-8.12 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is `stop` and a step fails, the JSON output `status` field SHALL be `\\\"error\\\"` | TEST-8.12 |\\n| AC-8.13 | Event | WHEN `on_error` is `continue`, the JSON output `status` field SHALL be `\\\"success\\\"` regardless of step failures | TEST-8.13 |\\n| AC-8.14 | State | WHILE `--quiet` is active, the system SHALL suppress all stderr output for workflow execution (same as recipe behavior). JSON stdout is unaffected | TEST-8.14 |\\n\\n### Non-Functional Requirements\\n\\n#### NFR-1: Deterministic Workflow Execution\\n\\nWorkflow execution must be deterministic — same inputs always produce the same outputs.\\n\\n**Acceptance Criteria (EARS):**\\n| ID | Type | Criterion | Traces To |\\n|----|------|-----------|-----------|\\n| AC-N1.1 | Ubiquitous | The system SHALL produce identical output when given the same workflow, variables, and existing files across multiple runs (extends I-1 to workflows) | TEST-N1.1 |\\n| AC-N1.2 | Ubiquitous | Conditional step evaluation (`when`) SHALL be deterministic — the same variables always produce the same skip/execute decision | TEST-N1.2 |\\n| AC-N1.3 | Ubiquitous | Variable mapping and override resolution SHALL be deterministic — the same workflow variables, vars_map, and vars always produce the same step variables | TEST-N1.3 |\\n\\n#### NFR-2: Ordered Step Execution\\n\\nSteps must execute in declaration order, and later steps must see filesystem changes from earlier steps.\\n\\n**Acceptance Criteria (EARS):**\\n| ID | Type | Criterion | Traces To |\\n|----|------|-----------|-----------|\\n| AC-N2.1 | Ubiquitous | The system SHALL execute workflow steps in declaration order, never in parallel or out of order (extends I-9 to workflows) | TEST-N2.1 |\\n| AC-N2.2 | Event | WHEN step N creates a file and step N+1 injects into that file, step N+1 SHALL find and operate on the file created by step N | TEST-N2.2 |\\n\\n#### NFR-3: Graceful Step Failure Reporting\\n\\nWhen a step fails, the error must include enough context for the caller (typically an LLM) to diagnose and fall back.\\n\\n**Acceptance Criteria (EARS):**\\n| ID | Type | Criterion | Traces To |\\n|----|------|-----------|-----------|\\n| AC-N3.1 | Event | WHEN a step fails, the error output SHALL include the step index (1-based), the recipe path, and the underlying structured error (what/where/why/hint) | TEST-N3.1 |\\n| AC-N3.2 | Event | WHEN a step's recipe operation fails, the rendered content SHALL be included in the error output so the caller can manually apply the change (extends I-4 and I-10 to workflows) | TEST-N3.2 |\\n| AC-N3.3 | Ubiquitous | Every workflow error SHALL map to the standard exit codes (1 for validation, 2 for rendering, 3 for file ops, 4 for variables) — no new exit codes are introduced | TEST-N3.3 |\\n\\n#### NFR-4: Idempotent Workflow Execution\\n\\nWorkflows must support safe re-runs when all constituent recipes use idempotency guards.\\n\\n**Acceptance Criteria (EARS):**\\n| ID | Type | Criterion | Traces To |\\n|----|------|-----------|-----------|\\n| AC-N4.1 | Event | WHEN a workflow where all steps use `skip_if` guards is run a second time with the same variables on already-modified files, the system SHALL report all operations across all steps as `\\\"action\\\": \\\"skip\\\"` | TEST-N4.1 |\\n| AC-N4.2 | Ubiquitous | The system SHALL not produce duplicate content when workflow steps use `skip_if` idempotency guards (extends I-2 to workflows) | TEST-N4.2 |\\n\\n## Interfaces\\n\\n### Public API (CLI)\\n\\n```\\n# New subcommand\\njig workflow <path> [options]\\n\\n# Extended commands (auto-detect workflow files)\\njig validate <path>     # auto-detects recipe vs workflow\\njig vars <path>         # auto-detects recipe vs workflow\\n\\n# Options (same as jig run)\\n  --vars <json>        Inline variables as JSON string\\n  --vars-file <path>   Variables from a JSON file\\n  --vars-stdin         Read variables from stdin\\n  --dry-run            Preview without writing files\\n  --json               Force JSON output to stdout\\n  --quiet              Suppress stderr output\\n  --force              Overwrite existing files\\n  --base-dir <path>    Resolve output paths from this directory\\n  --verbose            Include rendered content and diagnostics in output\\n```\\n\\n### Workflow YAML Format\\n\\n```yaml\\n# workflow.yaml\\nname: add-field                              # optional\\ndescription: \\\"Add a field across the stack\\\"  # optional\\n\\nvariables:                                   # optional\\n  app:\\n    type: string\\n    required: true\\n  model:\\n    type: string\\n    required: true\\n  field_name:\\n    type: string\\n    required: true\\n\\non_error: stop                               # optional, default: stop\\n\\nsteps:\\n  - recipe: ./model/add-field/recipe.yaml\\n  - recipe: ./service/add-method/recipe.yaml\\n    when: \\\"{{ update_service }}\\\"\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n23:{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_10\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\"sed -n '1,260p' AGENTS.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"# Mentat\\n\\nSpec-driven development workflow with dual-agent planning and iterative execution.\\n\\n## Key Documents\\n\\n- [docs/INVARIANTS.md](docs/INVARIANTS.md) - Project-wide constraints\\n- [docs/ARCHITECTURE.md](docs/ARCHITECTURE.md) - System architecture\\n- [docs/development/](docs/development/) - Methodology and philosophy\\n\\n## Workflow\\n\\n1. `/ws-init <name>` - Initialize workstream docs\\n2. `/ws-plan <name>` - Dual-agent planning (Claude + Codex), synthesize\\n3. `/ws-plan-review <name>` - Adversarial review of plan/specs\\n4. `/ws-execute <name> [task]` - Iterative execution with fresh-context retries\\n5. `/ws-validate` - Check task readiness (tests + VALIDATION.md)\\n6. `/ws-review <name> [task]` - Adversarial code review\\n7. `/ws-consolidate` - Capture learnings, update durable docs\\n\\n## Conventions\\n\\n- EARS format for all acceptance criteria in SPEC.md\\n- PLAN.md is source of truth for status/phases\\n- SPEC.md is canonical for requirements and interfaces\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n28:{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_13\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\"sed -n '1,260p' docs/ROADMAP.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"# ROADMAP.md\\n\\nFull delivery plan for jig, bridging the product spec (`jig.md`) to workstream execution. Every feature in the spec is accounted for here — either assigned to a milestone, explicitly deferred, or marked as cross-cutting infrastructure.\\n\\nLast updated: 2026-04-04\\n\\n## Current State\\n\\n| Milestone | Status | Tests | Docs |\\n|-----------|--------|-------|------|\\n| v0.1 core-engine | **Done** | 191 | PLAN, SPEC, SHARED-CONTEXT, NARRATIVE |\\n| v0.2 replace-patch | **Done** | 308 total | PLAN, SPEC, SHARED-CONTEXT, NARRATIVE |\\n| v0.3 workflows | **Done** | 343 total | PLAN, SPEC, SHARED-CONTEXT, NARRATIVE |\\n| v0.4–v1.0 | Described in jig.md | — | Nothing beyond one-line mentions in ARCHITECTURE.md |\\n\\nThe engine works. The planning infrastructure works. What's missing is the roadmap connecting the two through the remaining milestones.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Two Tracks\\n\\njig has two parallel development tracks with dependencies between them:\\n\\n```\\nENGINE TRACK (the tool itself)          AGENT TRACK (how agents use the tool)\\n─────────────────────────────           ──────────────────────────────────────\\nv0.1 core-engine ✅\\nv0.2 replace-patch ✅\\nv0.3 workflows ──────────────────────── agent evals (basic scenarios)\\nv0.4 libraries ──────────────────────── MCP server\\nv0.5 distribution                       project instructions template\\nv0.6 Claude Code plugin ◄────────────── requires MCP + libraries\\nv0.7 scan & check                       agent evals (full scenario suite)\\nv0.8 infer\\nv0.9 polyglot / schema-first\\nv1.0 stable\\n```\\n\\nThe engine track is sequential — each milestone builds on the previous. The agent track runs in parallel once the engine is usable (after v0.3), and the two converge at v0.6 (Claude Code plugin).\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Milestone Details\\n\\n### v0.3 — Workflows (next up)\\n\\n**Workstream docs:** Complete (PLAN, SPEC, SHARED-CONTEXT)\\n**Depends on:** v0.2 (done)\\n**jig.md reference:** lines 1223–1253, roadmap line 1920\\n\\nMulti-recipe orchestration. Chain recipes into a single `jig workflow` invocation with conditional steps (`when`), variable mapping (`vars_map`, `vars`), and error handling modes (`stop`, `continue`, `report`).\\n\\n**Scope (from SPEC):**\\n- Workflow YAML parsing (distinct from recipe format)\\n- `jig workflow` CLI command\\n- Conditional steps via Jinja2 template truthiness\\n- Variable mapping and overrides between steps\\n- Three error handling modes\\n- Per-step result reporting in JSON output\\n- `jig validate` and `jig vars` auto-detect workflow files\\n- 25 integration test fixtures\\n\\n**What this unlocks:** Workflows are the composition primitive. Without them, every multi-file change requires the agent to call `jig run` N times. With them, one `jig workflow` call handles the entire cascade. This is the minimum viable surface for meaningful agent evals.\\n\\n**Known blockers from v0.2:**\\n- `Position::Sorted` is a stub (panics) — any workflow step using it will fail\\n- `write_back` in replace.rs/patch.rs may swallow I/O errors\\n- 11 of 26 spec-required integration fixtures still missing\\n- `cmd_run` needs refactoring to extract reusable `run_recipe()` for workflow steps\\n\\n---\\n\\n### v0.4 — Libraries\\n\\n**Workstream docs:** None (needs PLAN + SPEC)\\n**Depends on:** v0.3 (workflows reference library recipes)\\n**jig.md reference:** lines 1059–1376, roadmap line 1929\\n\\nLibraries are versioned recipe collections for a framework. This is where framework opinions live — jig itself stays agnostic.\\n\\n**Scope (from jig.md):**\\n\\n*Library manifest:*\\n- `jig-library.yaml` format: name, version, description, framework, language\\n- `conventions` block: path templates mapping concerns to file locations\\n- `recipes` block: flat list of recipe paths + descriptions\\n- `workflows` block: multi-recipe workflow definitions (uses v0.3 workflow format)\\n\\n*CLI commands:*\\n- `jig library add <url|path>` — install from git repo or local directory\\n- `jig library remove <name>` — uninstall\\n- `jig library update <name>` — pull latest\\n- `jig library list` — show installed libraries\\n- `jig library recipes <name>` — list all recipes in a library\\n- `jig library info <name>/<recipe>` — show recipe details\\n- `jig library workflows <name>` — list workflows\\n\\n*Installation:*\\n- Global: `~/.jig/libraries/`\\n- Project-local: `.jig/libraries/` (takes precedence)\\n\\n*Convention overrides:*\\n- `.jigrc.yaml` per-project convention remapping\\n- Override just the path patterns, keep everything else\\n\\n*Project extensions:*\\n- `.jig/overrides/<library>/<recipe>/templates/` — replace a single template without forking\\n- `.jig/extensions/<library>/<recipe>/` — add new recipes namespaced under a library\\n\\n**What this unlocks:** Libraries make jig useful beyond a single project. They're also a prerequisite for the MCP server (which needs `jig_library_recipes` to work), the Claude Code plugin (which wraps a library), and meaningful agent evals (which need a library like jig-django to test against).\\n\\n**Open questions for SPEC:**\\n- Does `jig library add` from git clone the whole repo or just the jig-library.yaml + recipe dirs?\\n- Versioning strategy: git tags? semver in manifest? lock file?\\n- How does `jig run` resolve a library recipe path (e.g., `jig run django/model/add-field`)? Is it `jig run --library django model/add-field` or path-based?\\n- Does `jig workflow` accept library-qualified workflow names directly?\\n\\n---\\n\\n### v0.5 — Distribution\\n\\n**Workstream docs:** None (needs PLAN)\\n**Depends on:** v0.4 (distribution should include library install capability)\\n**jig.md reference:** lines 806–950, roadmap line 1937\\n\\nPackage and ship jig so others can install it.\\n\\n**Scope (from jig.md):**\\n\\n| Channel | Mechanism |\\n|---------|-----------|\\n| GitHub Releases | Cross-compiled binaries (macOS arm64/x86_64, Linux arm64/x86_64/musl, Windows x86_64) |\\n| Homebrew | Tap first (`brew tap <org>/tools`), graduate to homebrew-core at 50+ stars |\\n| Cargo | `cargo install jig-cli` on crates.io |\\n| Nix | Flake with `buildRustPackage` |\\n| npm | Thin binary wrapper (`npx @<org>/jig`), platform-specific postinstall (esbuild pattern) |\\n| Shell installer | `curl -fsSL .../install.sh \\\\| sh` — detects platform, downloads binary |\\n\\n*CI/CD:*\\n- GitHub Actions workflow for cross-compilation\\n- Release automation on git tag push\\n- Binary signing (optional)\\n- SHA256 checksums in release notes\\n\\n**What this unlocks:** External users. Everything before v0.5 is author-only usage. This is also when the npm wrapper becomes available, which matters because the MCP server ships as `npx @jig/mcp-server` and needs to find the `jig` binary.\\n\\n**Open questions for PLAN:**\\n- Minimum viable distribution: just GitHub Releases + Homebrew tap? Or all channels at once?\\n- Does the npm wrapper ship in this milestone or with the MCP server?\\n\\n---\\n\\n### v0.6 — Claude Code Plugin\\n\\n**Workstream docs:** None (needs PLAN + SPEC)\\n**Depends on:** v0.4 (libraries), MCP server (agent track)\\n**jig.md reference:** lines 650–730, 1307–1376, roadmap line 1946\\n\\nThe deepest integration: jig as a Claude Code plugin bundling MCP tools, skills, and hooks.\\n\\n**Scope (from jig.md):**\\n\\n*jig plugin itself:*\\n- `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` manifest\\n- `/jig:init` skill — scaffold recipe + templates dir inside a skill\\n- `/jig:doctor` skill — validate all recipes in a plugin\\n\\n*jig-django as reference library + plugin:*\\n- First community library: recipes for model, service, view, schema, admin, test, factory\\n- Workflows: `add-field`, `add-endpoint`, `scaffold-resource`\\n- Claude Code skills wrapping each workflow: read code → extract variables → call jig\\n- Dual-publish: installable as `jig library add` AND as a Claude Code plugin\\n\\n*Skill structure (from jig.md):*\\n```\\njig-django/\\n  jig-library.yaml              # library manifest\\n  .claude-plugin/plugin.json    # Claude Code plugin manifest\\n  skills/\\n    add-field/SKILL.md           # reads model, extracts context, runs jig workflow\\n    add-endpoint/SKILL.md\\n    scaffold-resource/SKILL.md\\n  model/add-field/recipe.yaml    # actual recipes\\n  service/add-method/recipe.yaml\\n  ...\\n```\\n\\n**What this unlocks:** The full vision — a developer says \\\"add a loyalty_tier field to Reservation\\\" and gets a consistent, multi-file, team-compliant change. The skill handles the intelligence (reading code, extracting variables), jig handles the mechanics (rendering, patching).\\n\\n**Open questions for SPEC:**\\n- Does the jig plugin provide the MCP server, or is it registered separately?\\n- Hook integration: what triggers `jig check` automatically? Post-edit on model files?\\n- How does dual-publish versioning work? Library version matches plugin version?\\n\\n---\\n\\n### v0.7 — Scan & Check\\n\\n**Workstream docs:** None (needs PLAN + SPEC)\\n**Depends on:** v0.4 (needs library recipes to scan/check against)\\n**jig.md reference:** lines 1435–1720, roadmap line 1961\\n\\nReverse operations that close the loop: instead of variables → files, go files → variables (scan) and files → conformance report (check).\\n\\n**Scope (from jig.md):**\\n\\n*`jig scan`:*\\n- Reverse a recipe: given an existing file, extract the variables that would have produced it\\n- File-level scan: `jig scan django/model ./hotels/models/reservation.py`\\n- Directory-level scan: `jig scan django ./hotels/` — project map showing coverage\\n- Output: JSON with `variables`, `confidence`, `unrecognized` (lines the recipe can't explain)\\n- Enables the workflow: scan → LLM modifies variables → run recipe\\n\\n*`jig check`:*\\n- Conformance verification: `jig check django/model ./hotels/models/*.py`\\n- Issue reporting with severity levels (error, warn)\\n- `fix_recipe` references: when an issue has a known recipe fix, jig tells you\\n- Directory-level checking across all library concerns\\n- `--strict` and `--exit-on-failure` flags for CI gating\\n- JSON output for LLM consumption\\n\\n**What this unlocks:** Scan → Check → Fix is a self-healing loop. The LLM can run `jig check`, read the issues, and automatically invoke the fix recipes. Check as CI gate turns team conventions into enforceable rules — the recipe IS the source of truth.\\n\\n**Open questions for SPEC:**\\n- Scan algorithm: template reverse-matching is complex. What's the minimum viable approach? Regex extraction from anchor patterns? Or full template inversion?\\n- Confidence scoring: what's the threshold for \\\"matched\\\"?\\n- How does scan handle files that deviate significantly from the recipe?\\n\\n---\\n\\n### v0.8 — Infer\\n\\n**Workstream docs:** None (needs PLAN + SPEC)\\n**Depends on:** v0.7 (scan provides the foundation for pattern recognition)\\n**jig.md reference:** lines 1526–1625, roadmap line 1968\\n\\nLearn recipes from examples instead of writing them by hand.\\n\\n**Scope (from jig.md):**\\n- `jig infer --before <file> --after <file>` — learn from a single before/after pair\\n- `jig infer --example <file>:before,after` (multiple) — variable detection improves with more examples\\n- `jig infer --from-git --pattern \\\"Add * field to *\\\"` — learn from commit history\\n- `jig infer --from-commit <sha>` — learn multi-file workflow from a single commit\\n- Draft/review/promote workflow: inferred recipes go to `_drafts/`, reviewed, then promoted\\n- Inferred recipes are expected to be 70-90% correct (the last 10-30% is human judgment)\\n\\n**What this unlocks:** The biggest friction reduction. Instead of hand-writing recipes, developers (or the LLM) infer them from the team's actual behavior. Combined with the observation engine (post-1.0), this makes recipe creation nearly zero-effort.\\n\\n---\\n\\n### v0.9 — Polyglot & Schema-First\\n\\n**Workstream docs:** None (needs PLAN + SPEC)\\n**Depends on:** v0.4 (libraries), v0.3 (workflows)\\n**jig.md reference:** lines 1722–1851, roadmap line 1975\\n\\nCross the framework boundary. A single command touches Django backend + Vue frontend.\\n\\n**Scope (from jig.md):**\\n- Cross-library workflows: steps reference different libraries (`library: django`, `library: vue`)\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n52:{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_25\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\"sed -n '1,260p' docs/workstreams/core-engine/PLAN.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"# PLAN.md\\n\\n> Workstream: core-engine\\n> Last updated: 2026-04-04\\n> Status: Complete\\n\\n## Objective\\n\\nDeliver the minimum viable jig CLI: parse recipes, validate variables, render Jinja2 templates, execute create and inject file operations, and report structured results. This is the v0.1 feature set — the smallest thing that lets an LLM call `jig run` to produce files from a recipe instead of re-deriving boilerplate.\\n\\nCovers ARCHITECTURE.md Phases A through E (skeleton, rendering, create, inject, integration tests).\\n\\n## Phases\\n\\n### Phase 1: Skeleton + Recipe Parsing\\nStatus: Complete\\nTraces to: FR-1, FR-7 (partial), NFR-4, NFR-5\\n\\nBootstrap the Rust crate, wire up clap, and make recipe parsing work end-to-end. After this phase, `jig validate` and `jig vars` are functional commands.\\n\\n#### Milestones\\n- [x] 1.1: Cargo.toml with dependencies (serde, serde_yaml, serde_json, clap, thiserror, regex, indexmap)\\n- [x] 1.2: `src/error.rs` — StructuredError struct (what/where/why/hint), JigError enum wrapping StructuredError with exit code mapping (0-4)\\n- [x] 1.3: `src/recipe.rs` — Recipe, VariableDecl, VarType, FileOp structs with serde deserialization; template path resolution relative to recipe location; structural validation (missing fields, missing template files); unknown operation type detection with clear error message; custom deserialization for FileOp — use intermediate flat struct with optional fields, then validate and convert to typed enum (reject when more than one of `to`/`inject`/`replace`/`patch` is present, or none is present; if `replace` or `patch` is present, emit AC-1.10 'not supported in v0.1' error, reject when multiple inject modes (after/before/prepend/append) are specified); compile-check regex patterns in after/before fields during recipe validation\\n- [x] 1.4: `src/variables.rs` — Variable merging and type-checking scaffolding (types imported from recipe.rs, validation logic added in Phase 2)\\n- [x] 1.5: `src/main.rs` — clap CLI with `validate` and `vars` subcommands, `#[command(version)]`; wire recipe parsing; map errors to exit codes; `jig validate` outputs summary to stderr (variable count, operation types); with `--json`, outputs structured JSON to stdout\\n- [x] 1.6: Unit tests for recipe parsing — valid recipe, missing fields, malformed YAML, missing template files, optional metadata\\n\\n#### Validation Criteria\\n- `jig validate recipe.yaml` parses the example recipe from jig.md and exits 0\\n- `jig validate bad.yaml` exits 1 with a clear error naming the problem\\n- `jig vars recipe.yaml` outputs JSON matching the SPEC schema (type, required, default, description)\\n- Template paths resolve relative to recipe file, not cwd\\n- AC-1.1 through AC-1.15 have corresponding unit tests (AC-1.11 empty files array tested in Phase 3 when `jig run` exists)\\n- `jig validate` output includes variable names and operation types\\n- AC-N5.1 exit codes are correct for recipe validation errors\\n\\n#### Key Files\\n- `Cargo.toml`\\n- `src/main.rs`\\n- `src/recipe.rs`\\n- `src/variables.rs` (types only)\\n- `src/error.rs`\\n\\n#### Dependencies\\n- regex crate (needed at parse time for compile-checking after/before patterns)\\n\\n---\\n\\n### Phase 2: Variable Validation + Template Rendering\\nStatus: Complete\\nTraces to: FR-2, FR-3, FR-7 (partial), NFR-1 (partial)\\n\\nFull variable pipeline (parse, merge, type-check) and template rendering with all 13 built-in filters. After this phase, `jig render` works.\\n\\n#### Milestones\\n- [x] 2.1: `src/variables.rs` — full validation: parse JSON from --vars/--vars-file/--vars-stdin; merge with precedence (defaults < file < stdin < inline); type-check against declarations; required field enforcement; enum validation; array item type validation\\n- [x] 2.2: `src/filters.rs` — all 13 built-in filters registered with minijinja: snakecase, camelcase, pascalcase, kebabcase, upper, lower, capitalize, replace, pluralize, singularize, quote, indent, join\\n- [x] 2.3: `src/renderer.rs` — minijinja Environment setup; template loading from recipe-relative paths; filter registration; render with variables context; structured error on undefined variable (with \\\"did you mean?\\\" via edit distance) and syntax errors (with file + line)\\n- [x] 2.4: Wire `render` subcommand in main.rs with --vars, --vars-file, --vars-stdin, --to options. For `jig render`, create a standalone Environment with filters registered but no template directory — load the template file directly by path via `render_str()` or equivalent\\n- [x] 2.5: Unit tests for variable validation — every VarType, required missing, default fallback, enum rejection, array item mismatch, merge precedence\\n- [x] 2.6: Unit tests + insta snapshot tests for all 13 filters and template rendering (conditionals, loops, comments, raw blocks, undefined vars, syntax errors)\\n\\n#### Validation Criteria\\n- All 13 filters produce correct output per AC-3.4 through AC-3.14\\n- `jig render template.j2 --vars '{\\\"class_name\\\": \\\"BookingService\\\"}'` renders to stdout\\n- Type mismatch exits 4 with expected vs actual type (AC-2.7)\\n- Missing required variable exits 4 with variable name and hint (AC-2.5)\\n- Merge precedence: inline --vars wins over --vars-stdin wins over --vars-file wins over defaults (AC-2.4)\\n- Undefined template variable exits 2 with \\\"did you mean?\\\" hint (AC-3.17)\\n- Template syntax error exits 2 with file path and line number (AC-3.18)\\n- Same inputs produce byte-identical output across runs (AC-N1.1)\\n- `jig render template.j2 --vars '...' --to output.txt` writes to file instead of stdout (AC-7.4)\\n- Multiple validation errors accumulated and reported together (AC-2.11)\\n\\n#### Key Files\\n- `src/variables.rs` (full implementation)\\n- `src/filters.rs`\\n- `src/renderer.rs`\\n\\n#### Dependencies\\n- heck crate for case conversions\\n- minijinja crate for template rendering\\n\\n---\\n\\n### Phase 3: Create Operation + Output Formatting\\nStatus: Complete\\nTraces to: FR-4, FR-6, FR-7 (complete), NFR-2 (partial), NFR-4, NFR-6\\n\\nFile creation with templated output paths, directory auto-creation, skip_if_exists, and dual-stream output (JSON stdout / human stderr). After this phase, `jig run` works for create-only recipes.\\n\\n#### Milestones\\n- [x] 3.1: `src/operations/mod.rs` — ExecutionContext struct (base_dir, dry_run, force, virtual_files for dry-run state); OpResult enum (Success with action/path/lines/location, Skip, Error); operation dispatch (create only initially). In dry-run mode, create ops populate virtual_files instead of writing to disk.\\n- [x] 3.2: `src/operations/create.rs` — render `to` path as template; create parent directories; write rendered content; skip_if_exists logic; --force override; --base-dir path resolution\\n- [x] 3.3: `src/output.rs` — OutputMode enum (Json/Human/Quiet); TTY auto-detection; JSON serialization of operations array + files_written/files_skipped; track per-path write/skip status for files_written and files_skipped arrays; colored human output to stderr; --verbose includes rendered content\\n- [x] 3.4: Wire `run` subcommand in main.rs with --dry-run, --json, --quiet, --force, --base-dir, --verbose; render all template contents AND templated path fields (`to`, `inject`, `skip_if`) into a Vec before executing any operation — rendering failures abort before any file is touched; execute operations in declaration order; fail-fast on operation errors — stop execution on first failure\\n- [x] 3.5: Unit tests for create operation — happy path, skip_if_exists, force overwrite, templated paths, directory creation, file-already-exists error\\n- [x] 3.6: Unit tests for output formatting — JSON schema matches SPEC, human output, quiet mode, verbose mode, dry-run\\n\\n#### Validation Criteria\\n- `jig run recipe.yaml --vars '...'` creates files at templated paths (AC-4.1, AC-4.2)\\n- Parent directories created automatically (AC-4.3)\\n- skip_if_exists: true skips existing files with action:\\\"skip\\\" (AC-4.4)\\n- Default (skip_if_exists: false) errors on existing file without --force (AC-4.5)\\n- --force overwrites regardless (AC-4.6)\\n- --base-dir changes output root (AC-4.7)\\n- --dry-run produces output but writes nothing (AC-6.8)\\n- JSON output when piped, human output when TTY (AC-6.1, AC-6.2)\\n- --json forces JSON (AC-6.3), --quiet suppresses non-errors (AC-6.4)\\n- Operations execute in declaration order (AC-N6.1)\\n- Second run with skip_if_exists: true reports all skips (AC-N2.1)\\n\\n#### Key Files\\n- `src/operations/mod.rs`\\n- `src/operations/create.rs`\\n- `src/output.rs`\\n- `src/main.rs` (run subcommand)\\n\\n#### Dependencies\\n- owo-colors crate for terminal coloring\\n\\n---\\n\\n### Phase 4: Inject Operation\\nStatus: Complete\\nTraces to: FR-5, NFR-2 (complete), NFR-6\\n\\nContent injection into existing files via regex anchoring. All injection modes: after, before, prepend, append, with at:first/last match selection and skip_if idempotency. This completes the v0.1 operation set.\\n\\n#### Milestones\\n- [x] 4.1: `src/operations/inject.rs` — read target file (or from virtual_files if target created in same dry-run); skip_if string search (skip_if string search must check virtual_files content when target was created in same dry-run); regex anchor matching (after/before); at:first/at:last match selection; prepend/append modes; render inject path as template; write modified content; inject ops update virtual_files with post-injection content for subsequent operations\\n- [x] 4.2: Wire inject dispatch into operations/mod.rs\\n- [x] 4.3: Unit tests for every injection mode — after (first match), after (last match), before, prepend, append, skip_if, missing target file error, regex no-match error, templated inject path\\n- [x] 4.4: Integration test: recipe with create + inject in same run (create file, then inject into it — tests ordered execution AC-N6.2)\\n\\n#### Validation Criteria\\n- after: content on line after first match (AC-5.1)\\n- before: content on line before first match (AC-5.2)\\n- prepend: content at start of file (AC-5.3)\\n- append: content at end of file (AC-5.4)\\n- at:last uses last match (AC-5.5), at:first (default) uses first (AC-5.6)\\n- skip_if: skips when string found in file, reports action:\\\"skip\\\" (AC-5.7)\\n- Regex no-match exits 3 with pattern, file path, hint (AC-5.8)\\n- Missing target file exits 3 (AC-5.9)\\n- Inject path renders as template (AC-5.11)\\n- Create-then-inject in same recipe works (AC-N6.2)\\n- Second run with skip_if shows all skips — no duplicate content (AC-N2.2)\\n- Inject success reports action:\\\"inject\\\" with path, location, line count (AC-5.10)\\n- at field ignored when prepend/append specified; after/before without regex exits 1 (AC-5.12, AC-5.13)\\n- Invalid regex pattern in after/before exits 1 (AC-5.14)\\n- Multiple inject modes (after+before etc.) specified exits 1 (AC-5.15)\\n- --force has no effect on inject operations (AC-5.16)\\n\\n#### Key Files\\n- `src/operations/inject.rs`\\n- `src/operations/mod.rs` (dispatch update)\\n\\n#### Dependencies\\n- regex crate\\n\\n---\\n\\n### Phase 5: Integration Test Framework\\nStatus: Complete\\nTraces to: All FRs and NFRs (validation layer)\\n\\nFixture-based integration test harness that makes adding new test cases a matter of adding a directory. Snapshot tests for all output formats. This phase validates the entire v0.1 pipeline end-to-end.\\n\\n#### Milestones\\n- [x] 5.1: Test harness in `tests/integration.rs` — fixture discovery; copy existing/ to temp dir; run jig as subprocess; diff output against expected/; assert JSON output against expected_output.json; assert exit code against expected_exit_code\\n- [x] 5.2: Fixtures for create operations — simple create, templated path, skip_if_exists, force overwrite, directory creation\\n- [x] 5.3: Fixtures for inject operations — after/before/prepend/append, at:first/at:last, skip_if\\n- [x] 5.4: Fixtures for error cases — missing vars, bad type, missing template, missing target file, regex no-match, malformed YAML, file exists without force\\n- [x] 5.5: Fixtures for combined operations — create + inject in one recipe, multi-file recipe, idempotency (run twice)\\n- [x] 5.6: insta snapshot tests for JSON output format, human output format, error message format\\n- [x] 5.7: Determinism test — run same recipe twice, assert byte-identical output (AC-N1.1)\\n\\n#### Validation Criteria\\n- `cargo test` runs all unit + integration + snapshot tests green\\n- Every operation mode has at least one fixture\\n- Every error exit code (1-4) has at least one fixture\\n- Adding a new test case requires only a new directory, no code changes. Integration fixtures are auto-discovered from directories (no code changes to add tests). Spec-level unit tests are named functions in `#[cfg(test)]` modules (e.g., `spec::fr1::ac_1_1`). The two layers serve different purposes.\\n- Idempotency fixture: second run produces all skips, no file changes\\n- Binary has no dynamic dependencies beyond system libc (verified via `otool -L` on macOS, `ldd` on Linux)\\n- Every `TEST-*` ID in SPEC.md has a corresponding test function named `spec::fr{N}::ac_{N}_{M}` or `spec::nfr{N}::ac_n{N}_{M}`\\n- Error fixtures assert that error JSON contains `what`, `where`, `why`, and `hint` fields (at least one fixture per exit code)\\n\\n#### Key Files\\n- `tests/integration.rs`\\n- `tests/fixtures/` (directory tree)\\n\\n#### Dependencies\\n- insta crate for snapshot testing\\n- assert_cmd or similar for subprocess testing (or raw std::process::Command)\\n\\n## Dependencies\\n\\n- **Depends on:** None (first workstream, greenfield)\\n- **Blocks:** replace-operation (v0.2), patch-operation (v0.2), workflows (v0.3), libraries (v0.4)\\n\\n## Key Decisions\\n\\n| Decision | Choice | Rationale |\\n|----------|--------|-----------|\\n| Operations as enum, not trait objects | `FileOp` enum with match dispatch | Only 2 variants in v0.1 (create, inject). Trait abstraction adds complexity for no benefit until user-defined operations exist, which isn't planned. (ARCHITECTURE D-4) |\\n| Render before execute | Template rendering happens before operation execution | Errors caught before any file touched; rendered content always available for error messages (ARCHITECTURE D-1, I-4, I-10) |\\n| Recipe-relative template resolution | Template paths relative to recipe file, not cwd | Keeps recipes self-contained and portable (ARCHITECTURE D-2, I-7). --base-dir only affects output paths. |\\n| No global state | Every function takes inputs as arguments | Trivial testing, guaranteed determinism (ARCHITECTURE D-5, I-1) |\\n| thiserror for error types | Use thiserror derive macros for JigError | Idiomatic Rust error handling with minimal boilerplate, good Display/Error trait impls |\\n| IndexMap for variables | Preserve declaration order in variable maps | Deterministic iteration order for vars output and error messages (I-1) |\\n| Inline unit tests + fixture integration tests | #[cfg(test)] modules in each source file; tests/fixtures/ for end-to-end | Unit tests co-located with code for fast iteration; fixture tests for pipeline validation without code changes |\\n| pluralize/singularize approach | Use the `pluralizer` crate | Full English pluralization rules without hand-rolling. Acceptable dependency for correctness. |\\n| \\\"did you mean?\\\" for undefined vars | `strsim` crate (Levenshtein distance) against declared variable names | minijinja doesn't provide hints. Hand-rolling edit distance serves I-10 (graceful degradation) — the error tells the caller exactly what to fix. |\\n| Filter ownership | Register all 13 filters ourselves, even for ones minijinja provides (replace, indent, join) | We control exact behavior. No surprises from upstream minijinja changes. Determinism (I-1) over convenience. |\\n| Render all templates upfront | Render ALL templates before ANY file write | Fail-fast, no partial writes. Templates reference variables not created files. (D-1) |\\n| Extra variables pass through | Don't error or warn on undeclared variables in input | LLMs construct variable objects with extra fields. Strictness here breaks real usage. |\\n| Fail-fast on operation errors | Stop on first operation failure | Later operations may depend on earlier ones (e.g., inject targets a prior create). (I-9, AC-N6.2) |\\n\\n## Risks / Open Questions\\n\\nAll resolved — see Resolved Questions below.\\n\\n## Resolved Questions\\n\\n- **pluralize/singularize**: Using `pluralizer` crate. Full English rules, no hand-rolling.\\n- **\\\"did you mean?\\\"**: Using `strsim` crate for Levenshtein distance against declared var names. minijinja doesn't provide this. Serves I-10.\\n- **minijinja filter compatibility**: Register all 13 filters ourselves. We own the behavior, upstream changes don't affect us. Serves I-1.\\n- **TTY detection**: `std::io::IsTerminal` (Rust 1.70+) works correctly. No external crate needed.\\n- **stdin variable reading**: Documented as single-purpose per invocation. Non-interactive design (I-3) makes this a non-issue.\\n- **Large file handling for inject**: Reads entire file into memory. Acceptable for source files. Documented as known limitation.\\n- **pluralizer irregular plurals**: Works for common irregulars (person→people, child→children). Acceptable coverage.\\n- **minijinja filter override**: Overriding built-in filters works. All 13 registered as custom filters.\\n\\n## Completion Summary\\n\\n- **Completed**: 2026-04-04\\n- **Tests**: 191 total (177 unit + 2 CLI integration + 12 fixture integration)\\n- **Review**: Dual-agent review cycle (Claude + Codex) came back clean — 0 Critical, 0 Major findings\\n- **All 5 phases delivered**: validate, vars, render, run (create + inject), full integration test suite\\n- **CLI commands working**: `jig validate`, `jig vars`, `jig render`, `jig run`\\n\\n## Execution Order\\n\\n```\\nPhase 1 ──► Phase 2 ──► Phase 3 ──► Phase 4 ──► Phase 5\\nskeleton     vars+render  create+output inject     integration\\n                                                    tests\\n```\\n\\nEach phase produces a working, testable artifact. No phase depends on \\\"wiring up later.\\\" The CLI grows incrementally:\\n- After Phase 1: `jig validate`, `jig vars`\\n- After Phase 2: + `jig render`\\n- After Phase 3: + `jig run` (create only)\\n- After Phase 4: + `jig run` (create + inject) — **v0.1 complete**\\n- After Phase 5: Full test coverage validates the release\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n60:{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_30\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\"rg -n \\\\\\\"jigrc|libraries|library\\\\\\\" src tests docs | sed -n '1,240p'\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"docs/workstreams/workflows/PLAN.md:9:Add multi-recipe orchestration to jig (v0.3). A workflow chains multiple recipes into a single invocation with conditional steps (`when`), variable mapping between steps (`vars_map`, `vars`), and configurable error handling (`on_error: stop|continue|report`). This is the prerequisite for libraries (v0.4), which organize workflows over library-namespaced recipes.\\ndocs/workstreams/workflows/PLAN.md:114:- **Blocks:** libraries (v0.4) — libraries define workflows over library-namespaced recipes and need the workflow execution engine.\\ntests/integration.rs:627:        // On macOS, only system libraries should appear.\\ndocs/workstreams/workflows/reviews/code-review-claude-20260404-200636.md:1:{\\\"type\\\":\\\"result\\\",\\\"subtype\\\":\\\"success\\\",\\\"is_error\\\":false,\\\"duration_ms\\\":613439,\\\"duration_api_ms\\\":682224,\\\"num_turns\\\":29,\\\"result\\\":\\\"Good — `Replace` does NOT have a `skip_if` field, so `run_recipe` correctly only handles skip_if for Inject and Patch.\\\\n\\\\nI now have everything I need. Here's the review:\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n**Verdict: Request Changes**\\\\n\\\\n## Critical (must fix before merge)\\\\n\\\\n**C1: `extract_rendered_from_error` returns the error description, not the rendered template content**\\\\n`src/workflow.rs:621-628`\\\\n\\\\n```rust\\\\nfn extract_rendered_from_error(err: &JigError) -> Option<String> {\\\\n    match err {\\\\n        JigError::TemplateRendering(se) | JigError::FileOperation(se) => {\\\\n            Some(se.what.clone())  // ← BUG: \\\\\\\"what\\\\\\\" is \\\\\\\"anchor pattern not found\\\\\\\", not the rendered content\\\\n        }\\\\n        _ => None,\\\\n    }\\\\n}\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nThis is called at line 519 to populate `StepResult::Error::rendered_content`, which propagates to the JSON output's `rendered_content` field. The spec says (AC-8.4, AC-N3.2): rendered_content should contain the actual rendered template so an LLM caller can fall back to manual editing (I-4, I-10). Instead, it contains the error description string.\\\\n\\\\nThe fix: extract from the last `OpResult::Error` in `partial_results` instead:\\\\n```rust\\\\nErr((e, partial_results)) => {\\\\n    let rendered = partial_results.last().and_then(|r| match r {\\\\n        OpResult::Error { rendered_content, .. } if !rendered_content.is_empty() => {\\\\n            Some(rendered_content.clone())\\\\n        }\\\\n        _ => None,\\\\n    });\\\\n    // ...\\\\n}\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nFor non-operation errors (variable validation, when-template failure), `rendered_content: None` is correct — there is no rendered content to extract. The current code incorrectly returns `Some(\\\\\\\"missing required variable 'name'\\\\\\\")` as rendered_content for those cases too.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## Major (should fix)\\\\n\\\\n**M1: `format_workflow_json` status logic breaks with per-step `on_error` overrides**\\\\n`src/output.rs:300-306`\\\\n\\\\n```rust\\\\nlet status = if !has_errors || exit_code == 0 {\\\\n    \\\\\\\"success\\\\\\\"\\\\n} else if exit_code == 3 && result.on_error == workflow::OnError::Report {\\\\n    \\\\\\\"partial\\\\\\\"\\\\n} else {\\\\n    \\\\\\\"error\\\\\\\"\\\\n};\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nThis checks `result.on_error` (the **workflow-level** default). When a step uses `on_error: report` as a per-step override but the workflow default is `stop`, `compute_workflow_exit_code` correctly returns 3, but the status incorrectly becomes `\\\\\\\"error\\\\\\\"` instead of `\\\\\\\"partial\\\\\\\"`. The LLM caller sees exit code 3 + status \\\\\\\"error\\\\\\\" — a contradiction since \\\\\\\"error\\\\\\\" implies the workflow halted but exit 3 means report-mode partial failure.\\\\n\\\\nFix: don't rely on `result.on_error` for the \\\\\\\"partial\\\\\\\" check:\\\\n```rust\\\\nlet status = if !has_errors || exit_code == 0 {\\\\n    \\\\\\\"success\\\\\\\"\\\\n} else {\\\\n    // Determine if any errored step used report mode\\\\n    let any_report = result.steps.iter().any(|s| matches!(s, StepResult::Error { .. }))\\\\n        && exit_code == 3;\\\\n    // But also check if we stopped early (stop mode with file-op error also gives exit 3)\\\\n    let stopped_early = result.steps.iter().last().map_or(false, |s| s.is_error())\\\\n        && result.steps.len() < total_steps;  // need total from workflow\\\\n    if any_report && !stopped_early { \\\\\\\"partial\\\\\\\" } else { \\\\\\\"error\\\\\\\" }\\\\n};\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nOr simpler: pass a flag from `compute_workflow_exit_code` indicating whether report-mode errors were present.\\\\n\\\\n**M2: `resolve_step_variables` panics on non-object JSON via `.unwrap()`**\\\\n`src/workflow.rs:369` and `src/workflow.rs:394`\\\\n\\\\n```rust\\\\nlet obj = vars.as_object_mut().unwrap();\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nIf `vars` is ever not a JSON object (e.g., `null`, array, string), this panics with no context. This is a public function. While `variables::collect_vars` should always return an object, defensive code here avoids an opaque panic. Return a `JigError::VariableValidation` instead.\\\\n\\\\n**M3: `cmd_run` and `run_recipe` are divergent copies of the same logic**\\\\n`src/main.rs:335-427` vs `src/workflow.rs:551-618`\\\\n\\\\nThe rendering pipeline (create env → render templates → render paths → render skip_if → execute ops) is duplicated. They're already subtly different:\\\\n- `cmd_run` returns early via `handle_early_error` for template failures (no partial results)\\\\n- `run_recipe` collects into a vec and returns `Err((error, vec![]))` for template failures\\\\n\\\\nIf a new operation type or rendering phase is added (e.g., for libraries v0.4), the change must be made in both places. Refactor `cmd_run` to call `run_recipe` and handle early errors at the CLI layer.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## Minor (nice to fix)\\\\n\\\\n**m1: Determinism unit test is trivially weak**\\\\n`src/workflow.rs:1215-1233`\\\\n\\\\n```rust\\\\nresults.push(format!(\\\\\\\"{:?}\\\\\\\", result.steps.len()));\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nThis only compares the Debug representation of a `usize` (e.g., `\\\\\\\"1\\\\\\\"` == `\\\\\\\"1\\\\\\\"`). It doesn't compare actual output content, file content, or operation results. A real determinism test should compare the `StepResult` variants and their contents across runs (or use the integration-level determinism test which is much stronger).\\\\n\\\\n**m2: `load_workflow` collects all validation errors but only reports the first**\\\\n`src/workflow.rs:303-306`\\\\n\\\\n```rust\\\\nlet (_, _, err) = validation_errors.into_iter().next().unwrap();\\\\nreturn Err(JigError::RecipeValidation(err));\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nIf steps 2 and 5 both reference invalid recipes, the user only sees the error for step 2. They fix it, re-run, and then see step 5. Reporting all errors at once would be more LLM-friendly. The errors are already collected — just use `JigError::VariableValidation(errors)` style or a new multi-error variant.\\\\n\\\\n**m3: No integration test for AC-7.8 (recipe passed to `jig workflow`)**\\\\nThe spec requires (AC-7.8): passing a recipe file to `jig workflow` should exit 1 with a message pointing to `jig run`. The code handles this correctly (`main.rs:510-519`), but there's no test fixture for it. Add an `error-workflow-is-recipe` fixture with a recipe.yaml and expected_exit_code=1.\\\\n\\\\n**m4: Early error handler creates misleading JSON**\\\\n`src/main.rs:473-507`\\\\n\\\\nPre-execution errors produce JSON with `\\\\\\\"steps\\\\\\\": [{\\\\\\\"recipe\\\\\\\": \\\\\\\"\\\\\\\"}]` — an empty recipe string and a fake step entry. An LLM caller might try to interpret this. For pre-execution errors (file not found, bad YAML, variable validation), consider `\\\\\\\"steps\\\\\\\": []` instead, with the error at the top level.\\\\n\\\\n**m5: `validate_workflow` sets `valid: true` for all steps unconditionally**\\\\n`src/workflow.rs:342-344`\\\\n\\\\n```rust\\\\nvalid: true, // already validated in load_workflow\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nThis is correct (load_workflow would have returned an error if any step was invalid), but it means `jig validate` never produces a per-step error report — if any recipe is invalid, the whole command fails before reaching the validation output. AC-7.5/AC-7.6 only require per-step output when validation succeeds, so this is spec-compliant, but a validation command that shows \\\\\\\"steps 1, 3 valid / step 2 invalid\\\\\\\" would be more useful.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## Strengths\\\\n\\\\n- **Clean architecture**: Parsing → variable resolution → condition evaluation → execution is well-separated. Each concern is testable in isolation.\\\\n- **Simultaneous `vars_map` renaming**: The snapshot-then-remove-then-insert approach at lines 372-389 correctly implements non-chaining rename semantics. The test at line 887 nails the tricky case.\\\\n- **ExecutionContext sharing across steps**: Single context for virtual_files, base_dir, and force flag is the right design. Dry-run chaining works correctly (tested at line 1140).\\\\n- **File type auto-detection**: The `detect_file_type` function cleanly handles all four cases (workflow, recipe, both, neither) and integrates smoothly into `jig validate` and `jig vars`.\\\\n- **Error handling structure**: Every error path through the step execution loop respects the effective `on_error` mode (step override or workflow default). The pattern at lines 440-453 is repeated consistently for all five failure points.\\\\n- **35 unit tests + 25 integration fixtures**: Good coverage of core logic. The fixture auto-discovery pattern is elegant — no test registration needed.\\\\n- **Spec traceability**: The test names and comments reference acceptance criteria consistently.\\\",\\\"stop_reason\\\":\\\"end_turn\\\",\\\"session_id\\\":\\\"8f910b25-063b-4054-a75d-988d5e1469d4\\\",\\\"total_cost_usd\\\":2.344686099999999,\\\"usage\\\":{\\\"input_tokens\\\":20,\\\"cache_creation_input_tokens\\\":111076,\\\"cache_read_input_tokens\\\":1279858,\\\"output_tokens\\\":24985,\\\"server_tool_use\\\":{\\\"web_search_requests\\\":0,\\\"web_fetch_requests\\\":0},\\\"service_tier\\\":\\\"standard\\\",\\\"cache_creation\\\":{\\\"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens\\\":111076,\\\"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens\\\":0},\\\"inference_geo\\\":\\\"\\\",\\\"iterations\\\":[],\\\"speed\\\":\\\"standard\\\"},\\\"modelUsage\\\":{\\\"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001\\\":{\\\"inputTokens\\\":209,\\\"outputTokens\\\":16306,\\\"cacheReadInputTokens\\\":1611206,\\\"cacheCreationInputTokens\\\":114358,\\\"webSearchRequests\\\":0,\\\"costUSD\\\":0.38580709999999996,\\\"contextWindow\\\":200000,\\\"maxOutputTokens\\\":32000},\\\"claude-opus-4-6[1m]\\\":{\\\"inputTokens\\\":20,\\\"outputTokens\\\":24985,\\\"cacheReadInputTokens\\\":1279858,\\\"cacheCreationInputTokens\\\":111076,\\\"webSearchRequests\\\":0,\\\"costUSD\\\":1.958879,\\\"contextWindow\\\":1000000,\\\"maxOutputTokens\\\":64000}},\\\"permission_denials\\\":[{\\\"tool_name\\\":\\\"Bash\\\",\\\"tool_use_id\\\":\\\"toolu_01Buj5C7L5RAzHW4Ejikn4va\\\",\\\"tool_input\\\":{\\\"command\\\":\\\"for dir in /Users/tylerobriant/code/tetra/jig/tests/fixtures/error-workflow-* /Users/tylerobriant/code/tetra/jig/tests/fixtures/workflow-*; ls -1 \\\\\\\"$1\\\\\\\" 2>/dev/null' _ {} \\\\\\\\;\\\"}},{\\\"tool_name\\\":\\\"Bash\\\",\\\"tool_use_id\\\":\\\"toolu_01DT748GfevoWkkcFPQ1DT2i\\\",\\\"tool_input\\\":{\\\"command\\\":\\\"ls -1d /Users/tylerobriant/code/tetra/jig/tests/fixtures/workflow-* /Users/tylerobriant/code/tetra/jig/tests/fixtures/error-workflow-* | wc -l\\\"}}],\\\"terminal_reason\\\":\\\"completed\\\",\\\"fast_mode_state\\\":\\\"off\\\",\\\"uuid\\\":\\\"d44e2820-dc14-4775-8c5d-dd6f6cf3ac39\\\"}\\ndocs/workstreams/libraries/exec/codex-plan-20260404-213100.md:5:{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.completed\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_0\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"agent_message\\\",\\\"text\\\":\\\"I’ll produce a phase-by-phase implementation plan for workstream `libraries` aligned to your invariants and architecture. First I’m going to scan the repo for existing workstream/planning docs and any current library-related code so the plan maps to actual files and avoids duplicating prior decisions.\\\"}}\\ndocs/workstreams/libraries/exec/codex-plan-20260404-213100.md:8:{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.completed\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_2\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"agent_message\\\",\\\"text\\\":\\\"I found the project root and confirmed this branch already contains workflow-related work through Phase H. Next I’ll inspect `docs/` and current `src/library` state to anchor the `libraries` plan to existing structure and naming.\\\"}}\\ndocs/workstreams/libraries/exec/codex-plan-20260404-213100.md:15:{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.completed\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_6\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"agent_message\\\",\\\"text\\\":\\\"I located existing workstream plans/specs (`core-engine`, `replace-patch`, `workflows`) and there isn’t a `libraries` directory yet. I’m now reading prior `PLAN.md` style so this new execution plan matches established structure and depth.\\\"}}\\ndocs/workstreams/libraries/exec/codex-plan-20260404-213100.md:17:{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.completed\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_7\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"command_execution\\\",\\\"command\\\":\\\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\\\\\"sed -n '1,260p' docs/workstreams/workflows/PLAN.md\\\\\\\"\\\",\\\"aggregated_output\\\":\\\"# PLAN.md\\\\n\\\\n> Workstream: workflows\\\\n> Last updated: 2026-04-04\\\\n> Status: Complete\\\\n\\\\n## Objective\\\\n\\\\nAdd multi-recipe orchestration to jig (v0.3). A workflow chains multiple recipes into a single invocation with conditional steps (`when`), variable mapping between steps (`vars_map`, `vars`), and configurable error handling (`on_error: stop|continue|report`). This is the prerequisite for libraries (v0.4), which organize workflows over library-namespaced recipes.\\\\n\\\\nThe deliverable is: `jig workflow <path> --vars '{...}'` executes an ordered sequence of recipe invocations, with per-step JSON results, shared filesystem state, and predictable error behavior.\\\\n\\\\n## Phases\\\\n\\\\n### Phase 1: Workflow Parsing & CLI Detection\\\\nStatus: Complete\\\\n\\\\nBuild the workflow YAML parser and extend `jig validate` / `jig vars` to auto-detect workflow files.\\\\n\\\\n#### Milestones\\\\n- [x] 1.1: Create `src/workflow.rs` with `Workflow`, `WorkflowStep`, `OnError` structs and serde deserialization from YAML\\\\n- [x] 1.2: Implement `load_workflow()` — parse YAML, resolve recipe paths relative to workflow dir, validate recipe files exist and are structurally valid\\\\n- [x] 1.3: Implement auto-detection in `jig validate`: read YAML, check for `steps` vs `files` key, dispatch to workflow or recipe validation\\\\n- [x] 1.4: Implement auto-detection in `jig vars`: same detection logic, output workflow variable declarations\\\\n- [x] 1.5: Add workflow validation JSON and human output formats (type, name, variables, steps with recipe/valid/conditional)\\\\n- [x] 1.6: Unit tests for parsing: valid workflow, empty steps, missing steps key, both steps+files, bad on_error, missing recipe file, invalid recipe, no variables, optional metadata\\\\n\\\\n#### Validation Criteria\\\\n- `jig validate workflow.yaml` auto-detects and validates a workflow with 3+ steps, reporting each recipe's validity\\\\n- `jig vars workflow.yaml` outputs the workflow's variable declarations as JSON\\\\n- All parse-time error cases produce exit code 1 with structured error messages\\\\n- 20+ unit tests covering AC-1.1 through AC-1.20\\\\n\\\\n### Phase 2: Variable Resolution & Conditional Steps\\\\nStatus: Complete\\\\n\\\\nImplement step variable resolution (shared vars → vars_map → vars overrides) and `when` condition evaluation.\\\\n\\\\n#### Milestones\\\\n- [x] 2.1: Implement `resolve_step_variables()` — start with workflow vars, apply vars_map renaming (simultaneous, not chained), apply vars overrides\\\\n- [x] 2.2: Implement `evaluate_when()` — render Jinja2 template with workflow vars, apply falsy rules (empty, \\\\\\\"false\\\\\\\" case-insensitive, \\\\\\\"0\\\\\\\")\\\\n- [x] 2.3: Implement workflow-level variable validation — reuse `variables::validate_variables()` with workflow's `VariableDecl` map\\\\n- [x] 2.4: Implement step-level variable validation — validate step's resolved vars against the step recipe's `VariableDecl` map\\\\n- [x] 2.5: Unit tests for: vars_map rename semantics, vars_map with nonexistent source, vars_map target collision, vars override, combined vars_map+vars, when truthy/falsy cases, when with Jinja2 control flow, when with undefined variable, simultaneous vars_map\\\\n\\\\n#### Validation Criteria\\\\n- `resolve_step_variables()` produces correct output for all documented edge cases (AC-4.1 through AC-4.10)\\\\n- `evaluate_when()` correctly classifies: empty → false, \\\\\\\"false\\\\\\\"/\\\\\\\"False\\\\\\\"/\\\\\\\"FALSE\\\\\\\" → false, \\\\\\\"0\\\\\\\" → false, \\\\\\\"true\\\\\\\" → true, \\\\\\\"yes\\\\\\\" → true, non-empty string → true\\\\n- Variable validation errors at workflow level halt before any execution (exit 4)\\\\n- 15+ unit tests covering FR-3 and FR-4\\\\n\\\\n### Phase 3: Execution Engine\\\\nStatus: Complete\\\\n\\\\nBuild the workflow execution loop that iterates steps, evaluates conditions, resolves variables, and runs recipes — sharing filesystem state (and virtual_files in dry-run) across steps.\\\\n\\\\n#### Milestones\\\\n- [x] 3.1: Implement `execute_workflow()` — sequential step loop with condition evaluation, variable resolution, and recipe execution\\\\n- [x] 3.2: Extract `run_recipe()` in `workflow.rs` — reusable recipe execution without CLI concerns (rendering, operation execution, error collection)\\\\n- [x] 3.3: Implement cross-step `virtual_files` carryover for `--dry-run` mode — a single `ExecutionContext` spans all steps\\\\n- [x] 3.4: Implement `on_error` handling: stop (halt on failure), continue (record and proceed, exit 0), report (record and proceed, exit 3)\\\\n- [x] 3.5: Implement per-step `on_error` overrides — step-level value takes precedence over workflow-level default\\\\n- [x] 3.6: Implement exit code selection via `compute_workflow_exit_code()` in `main.rs`\\\\n- [x] 3.7: Unit tests for execution: basic two-step, conditional skip, chain create→inject, chain create→patch, error stop, error continue, error report, step on_error override, dry-run chaining\\\\n\\\\n#### Validation Criteria\\\\n- A two-step workflow where step 1 creates a file and step 2 injects into it succeeds in both normal and dry-run mode\\\\n- A workflow with `when: \\\\\\\"{{ false }}\\\\\\\"` skips the step and reports status \\\\\\\"skipped\\\\\\\"\\\\n- `on_error: stop` halts after first failure; `continue` runs all steps; `report` runs all steps but exits 3\\\\n- Per-step on_error correctly overrides workflow default\\\\n- 20+ unit tests covering FR-5 and FR-6\\\\n\\\\n### Phase 4: CLI Command & Output Formatting\\\\nStatus: Complete\\\\n\\\\nAdd the `jig workflow` subcommand and implement workflow-specific JSON and human output.\\\\n\\\\n#### Milestones\\\\n- [x] 4.1: Add `Workflow` variant to `Commands` enum in `main.rs` — accepts path + same global options as `Run`\\\\n- [x] 4.2: Implement `cmd_workflow()` in `main.rs` — parse workflow, validate vars, execute, format output, return exit code\\\\n- [x] 4.3: Implement `format_workflow_json()` in `output.rs` — per-step results, aggregate files_written/files_skipped, top-level workflow/on_error/status/dry_run fields\\\\n- [x] 4.4: Implement `format_workflow_human()` in `output.rs` — step headers (\\\\\\\"Step 1/3: ...\\\\\\\"), nested operation lines, summary line\\\\n- [x] 4.5: Handle edge case: `jig workflow` passed a recipe file → exit 1 with helpful message pointing to `jig run`\\\\n- [x] 4.6: Wire `--verbose` to include rendered content across all steps in both JSON and human output\\\\n- [x] 4.7: CLI integration tests: basic workflow run, JSON output format, human output format, --dry-run,
